name: The Johns Hopkins Hullabaloo
description: Johns Hopkins University · Baltimore · 9 Veritas Vos Liberabit The 1876

name: Bitter, Kenneth Oscar
description: There was a man from our town And he thought himself wondrous wise; He tried to raise a bramble-bush Not far beneath his eyes. And just as he saw that bush full-grown Which gave his colleagues pain He found himself beneath the blade And the growth shaved off again. Dear Cousin: I have just received your letter complimenting me upon my achievements, and I must say that it makes me feel brisk when I read the fine things you have written about me. It seems that the older I grow the greater the honors that are thrust upon me. Last fall when the battalion was reorganized they made me the Senior Captain, although I have never been able to decide why they did so. I think that the fierce-looking moustache which I was wearing at that time must have had a great influence upon the decision. By the way, I don’t wear the moustache now, for the girls admired it so much that I became bored and requested some of my friends to assist me in removing it. I am afraid that I didn't learn much while on our geology trip for we wore our uniforms and the girls simply went wild over us. The Bedford Springs girls were especially nice, and after going there I lost all interest in trilobites, echinoderms, and all other fossils. An officer under government supervision doesn’t have time for dead things, especially if he is a member of the Big Three. Not long after the geology trip the senior engineers were taken to Pittsburgh and we wore our uniforms again. When I led a party of my men through the different plants I certainly caused a commotion. The girls all stopped work to look at me and I heard a man say that I must have brought my men to stop a strike. Lampe, my lieutenant, had worked in Pittsburgh so he took the officers on the trip to a dance at the Westinghouse Club. I met several nice girls there and have decided to alternate my correspondence between Pittsburgh and Bedford Springs. Your charming cousin, Kenneth Oscar.

name: Bryan, Cecil Austin
motto: "No man can enjoy happiness without thinking that he enjoys it."—Johnson.
description: BRYAN is unique. To our knowledge he is the first one who has blended studies with girls and has done it with complete success. It may be because he neither smokes nor chews. In the classroom he is undeniably good, but it is with the ladies that he shines. He knows more of them intimately than we see pass Lexington Street and Park Avenue during the noon hour. This popularity may be due to his neatness, for his tie is always straight and his trousers always keenly pressed. Then we sometimes believe it is due to his first name. Cecil is such a smooth, rounded word that will slip off the tongue with the minimum amount of effort, and can be whispered ever so low. Besides everyone named Cecil is such a gallant. There is one characteristic of women that he seems to have borrowed. He always has a secret to impart, or at least it seems so. Almost any time you may see him talking apart in low tones to someone. Yet on the other hand entirely unlike his fair friends Cecil has a rare sense of humor. He can see a joke before it bites him, and register merriment by wrinkling up his face, and permitting a wide grin to gradually spread over his countenance. But he always grins widest at his own jokes. He is said to be a good singer, a joker, an expert in military affairs, and a philosopher. We give him credit for having an interest in a variety of things and being able to attend to all of them.

name: Canton, Eugene Jude
motto: "The fattest hog in Epicurus' sty." —William Mason.
description: CANTON was the most humorous man at Hopkins during his time. His humor was both apparent and hidden. His apparent humor consisted in his bulky frame, and his hidden humor in his natural Irish wit. Those who were more intimately acquainted with him were able to find out his ideals of life, and those were to eat without bound, and sleep without limit. He was able to sleep twenty-eight hours of the day. His best friends were cooks, waiters, and soda dispensers. But as much as he liked to eat and sleep, he would rather argue than do either of the above two things. Prohibition and Billy Sunday were his mortal enemies. Congressman Meeker was his idol. His activities, however, were not limited merely to sleeping and eating. He came very near to developing into an all around (or all round) athlete. Many good athletic judges at the university after watching his efforts on the gridiron in behalf of the Sophomore Class in 1916, when he pushed little Cort through the Freshman line, have declared him to be the greatest battering ram that ever came to Johns Hopkins. His Irish temperament would not let him keep out of the war, and he has enlisted in the Signal Corps. He is trying hard to get a commission, and if size counts for anything, he is going to be a general. From the last reports, the Q. M. has spent 100 hours in labor and many dollars in telegrams trying to find a uniform to fit him. We would advise the Q. M. to order an officer's uniform at once, as we feel certain Gene is going to get it anyway. Lord help Kaiser "Bill" when "Gene" sits on him. Here is good luck to the best of all fellows.

name: Jennings P. Chu
motto: "Tell you of the future; why, we do not understand the present."—Confucius.
description: There was a hunter who went hunting in the woods. In the distance he saw an object. He thought it was a bear but decided to go closer. At a close range he saw that it was a man. Then going still closer he saw that the man was his brother. When the two brothers met they embraced and went on rejoicing, each glad that they had not shot when they had sighted each other. It is with this little story that we express our relation with J. P. Chu. When he first came in our midst we saw him as a stranger from a far country; a representative of different morals, customs and language. On closer range we found that he was human, that some days he got high marks and others he didn't, that he could laugh, talk and say a merry good morning. Then we opened our arms and accepted him as a brother. Here is hoping that he may remain with us and send many more over from his country. But as a word to the wise, we would advise him to send some of the other sex to Goucher, because Dame Rumor has it that the Chinese delegation there now, is far out-numbered by the one at Hopkins and thus has a hard time with its studies because of the overwhelming demand of the Hopkins men.

name: Millard James Cover
motto: "When you are down in the mouth remember Jonah — he came out all right." — Magreene.
description: The deacon calls him Millard. The girls call him Jonah. The fellows call him Jimmie. The editors agree that once more man's reason must bow before woman's intuition; we unanimously vote for the second name. Jonah has had the distinction since his freshman days of being our fashion model. He has never been known to come to college until he has spent many hours on his toilet. In fact true to his namesake he always looks as though he just came out of the belly of a whale. It may be that the haggard appearance comes from his "parties" with Wells and that crowd—but we are getting ahead of ourselves. No doubt the one great thing in Jonah's college career was his downfall. When he came to us he was a meek and innocent lad. Alas! This lasted only a year. He met the Wells-Michelson combination. This pair could ruin anything "the likeness of which is in the heavens above, the earth beneath or the waters under the earth." Well—Jonah was like something in the heavens above. Class pride forbids our saying into what category Wells and Michelson put him. He has degenerated; he even carries matches, a knife, and when very, very angry lets slip an occasional "damn." Jonah may be said to be, however, as far as he is known outside the confines of this combination, a good fellow, unusually good natured, and a faithful friend.

name: Cullom, Kenneth Stetson
motto: "I have found you an argument; I am not obliged to find you an understanding."—Boswell.
clubs: Member R. O. T. C., S.
description: "Noodles" will be stamped upon graduation as proficient in applied and practical dreaming. Yet the words "applied dreaming" are not used advisedly for up to the present time not the slightest trace of logic has been found in any of his arguments—and it is in polemics that he indulges himself most. His activities in this field, furthermore, will cause him to be ever remembered by his classmates who were fortunate enough to take a course in logic with him and hear him prove "All S is P" or otherwise. "Noodles looks very much like a skeleton—yet we must say that he is a very animated skeleton—we wrestled with him once. The most prominent part of his frame are his legs. To these "Noodles" has diligently and successfully applied the difficult art of camouflage, because these members have the uncanny faculty of assuming positions of more or less angularity which when viewed from the rear look just like saplings. We caught a photo of those legs once when "Noodles" was in uniform standing among a small group of birches and we have never been able to distinguish "Noodles" from the birches. This unique character is aspiring to become a mechanical engineer. We know he will succeed if he keeps out of arguments. Should he be able to do this we predict that he will produce a "gas wagon" that will make the Liberty Motor look like Dick Benson's Ford.

name: Downey, John
motto: "Like Patience on a monument smiling at grief." — Shakespeare.
description: He came to us as a raw country product, hailing from Sandy Springs, Montgomery County. In Downey's freshman year he was a great favorite of Dr. Shenton, but we no longer hold this against him. Downey started to become civilized early in his college life. When a freshman he joined Teddy Kistler's dancing class. He became a knight of the ballroom. It is said as early as this Downey began to be a heart-breaker. After John's first year he disappeared from the sight of the writer and plunged into the mysteries of engineering. We have learned that even though Downey was deeply interested in his work he found time to go home once in a while to plow, set out plants and straighten things up. In later years he became a figure at the battalion dances and a frequent visitor at Goucher College and Kernan's. Taking all in all John certainly has budded out. Good-bye, good luck and the best of success, Montgomery.

name: Eby, James Brian
clubs: Lieutenant R. O. T. C., S.
description: We are required to discuss Lieutenant J. B. Eby, R. O. T. C., extant. From reports that have come to us, he makes a pretty good officer, but, of course, we do not know. The subject under discussion does not like class room work since America declared war upon Germany, and shows his dislike very plainly. He shows his ardent patriotism by gloriously flunking a German exam. If we were able to read his mind during a German hour, we would doubtless see there a great battle going on in the first line trenches—we would see an intricate puzzle of communicating trenches and supports, barbed wire entanglements (spider webs), and shell holes. The enemy is German, the wounds are inflicted by the instructor. Eby has one fault which must be considered. He is said to be getting fonder of the ladies. It is a fault that is not altogether bad and may explain some of the mental wars waged when he should be studying. At each battalion dance he appears with a different partner. If this continues we will be compelled to envy him. With all his likes and dislikes he seems to be a pretty good fellow. Within a year he expects to be marching into Berlin. If that is his ambition we will hold nothing against him.

name: Ewald, Harry William
motto: "Ambition is the only power that controls love."—Colley Cibber.
clubs: Signal Corps Radio School at College Park
description: HARRY BILL, the man with the wild look. He smiles as long as you agree with him, but just oppose him and his hair stands on end, and his eyes flash fire. One day during such a scene in Dr. Kouwenhoven's class he was even heard to emit a growl, whereupon someone in the class recommended the dog catchers. But this terrible look never goes out with Ewald on Wednesday or Sunday nights, for then he journeys west on the North Avenue car-line to see his superior officer—Sergeant Spike. We have heard that he follows wherever she leads. It's great to have a good officer. Harry is a great fellow and we like him immensely; we also like his rooms on Dolphin Street. Many a night the "engineering bunch" has gathered there for freshman parties, great feeds, and successful plotting. This apartment witnessed the laying out of the most successful plan of the E. E. Seniors, whereby the senior captain lost his nine by eleven scrub brush. It was a wonderful feat and was celebrated the next night by an all-night session. Harry was one of the Senior Captain's men, but Doc Kouwenhoven rescued him from this misfortune, and had him placed in the Signal Corps Radio School at College Park. We sympathize with the Sergeant, with the hope that her suffering may be alleviated we quote a happy thought expressed by the Royal Jester: The House of Canton: "If Harry Bill sends Kaiser Bill one of those wild looks, then he will soon be back again!"

name: Fonaroff, Frank Israel
motto:
"While timorous knowledge stands considering,
Audacious ignorance hath done the deed."—Daniel.
clubs: Member R. O. T. C., S.
description: Frank I. Fonaroff, exponent of the art of receiving a college degree with the least amount of effort. "Fonny" is a man of aspirations—the greatest of which is to get a job which does not require him to begin work until 10 A. M. We wish him luck and hope he will succeed in obtaining such employment for he will then be able to get up in time for breakfast. In one of his morning courses he has been able to adopt a definite program. This is in the course in fossils where among many others "The Old Man of the Mountain" is daily exhibited. "Fonny" now enters geology twenty minutes after the beginning of the hour with his arms filled with books, an overcoat, some loose papers, and a paper bag filled with sandwiches. Later in the lecture when the moving pictures begin "Fonny" takes out a sandwich and begins to eat his breakfast. In his other courses "Fonny" has mapped out a similar proceeding, so that his professors do not expect him until the hour is well advanced. "Fonny's" chief virtues are agreeing with anything that is said and serving as the best man at the weddings of his friends. It is said that one of "Fonny's" adventures furnished the basis of that famous story, entitled "The Best Man and the Cheese," so current in the chem. laboratory several years ago.

name: Archibald Golder
motto: "O happiness! Our being's end and aim! Good, pleasure, ease, content! whate'er thy name! That something still which prompts the eternal sigh, For which we bear to live, or dare to die."---Pope.
description: G stands for Golder as well as for Girl. In other words, Archie shines in the gentle art of fussing. He runs second only to W. J. H. B. X. Y. Z. P. D. Q. Wells. The distinction lies in the fact that Golder picks his, while Wells takes his just as they come. For example, we would drop over dead if we heard that Archie was seen with a "chorine" at the Burlesque Boys' Ball. We would be equally dumbfounded if we were told that Wells was chummy with a Green Spring Valley million-heiress. Reverse the cases, however, and we feel at home. So do Wells and Golder. We have to hand it to Archie for one thing---his perseverance. For four years he has practised faithfully with the football, tracks, and lacrosse teams; and though he was not always successful in gaining a place on the Varsity, he won the admiration of every coach for his gameness and stamina. Good boy, Archie!

name: Clayton Morris Hall
motto: "It is only during the session of sweet silent thought that a man can summon his soul to judgment." — Elbert Hubbard.
description: Well, who would have thought it? We never suspected for a moment that Morris had any such reason for his inordinate quietness and studiousness until one day we caught him reading this part of Elbert Hubbard's philosophy. We had always thought that he wanted a good average or just had nothing to say or even — in the spring time — suspected he was in love. But who ever imagined that Morris had a soul. Surely he doesn't look it. But perhaps from the heights of Hubbardian or Platonic philosophy he has been secretly laughing at the puny attempts of us school boys. But why should he summon his soul to judgment? Could he have done something for which he might be ashamed? The only thing which we could imagine is that he is going to be a professor. Not that we have anything against professors — ye gods know! But there seems to be an idea prevailing that there are too many already. We are glad, however, to have some studious members in 1918. It gives the local color which is necessary for the general public. So good luck to you, Morris, and to Elbert in the bargain!

name: Adolf Lawrence Hamburger
motto: "I may have been wrong at times in my life, but I don't believe it."
description: If Hamburger is ever captured by the Germans, and if they desire to demonstrate their best brand of schrecklichkeit, they will sever connections between his tongue and his vocal cords. We can think of nothing which will prove more crippling and demoralizing to the class' foremost advocate of free speech. Adolf was cut out for either a fish dealer or a statesman; and so far we have been unable to decide which profession would do him the greater justice. Surely, nothing could be more picturesque than Hamburger wheeling before him a greasy, savory push-cart; but on the other hand, it seems to us that his master diplomacy and sterling statesmanship, his powers of argumentation and his keen foresightedness, deserve some post where they can be brought forth to the greatest advantage — for example, the Vice-Presidency of the United States, or the Mayoralty of Pikesville. The Battalion lost the opportunity of adding a high-class man to its numbers when it committed the fatal blunder which led to Hamburger's reconsidering his decision to join the ranks. When the announcement was made that the contract for the uniforms had been awarded to Isaac Hamburger and Sons, it was too much for Adolf. Gone was his idea of the glory and distinction of the military man. How could he look distinguished if his uniform did not fit him? On the first day that the Battalion lined up in its new Hamburger uniforms, Adolf stood off to one side and looked sadly down the files. Then he turned to the man beside him and said in a choking voice, "War is Hell."

name: Harris, George Sylvester
motto: "On their merits modest men are dumb."—George Coleman.
description: WHANG! Out popped the circuit breaker and immediately the E. E. laboratory was filled with Harris' well-known battle cry, "Lampe did it." To be sure George made the faulty connection, but his motto seems to be "Have an Alibi," and he has one for every occurrence that might involve him. This is one of George's big assets. Another is that he is in one of two places when out of class—either in the Major's office or on his way there. In this respect he resembles the ex-Czar of the battalion, Instructor Captain Coon(e). The experiences of Harris are many: As a night telephone operator in his home town he discovered that small town life for some people is more than it is cracked up to be; as a handy man on a schooner he learned to tie knots, whether he can untie them will soon be seen from the Frostburg knot that now entangles him; as a shipyard worker, he discovered overtime pads the pay envelope and requires no work except keeping out of sight; and as a surveyor it is understood he saw more through his telescope than pegs and rods, especially when surveying on Charles Street; as a student he discovered that it pays to work hard, but that a gas attack on instructors helps out a lot. There is one noticeable thing wrong with George and that is his height. George with his masterly head contraption literally waded through the mental exams, but lacked three-quarters of an inch in height to please Uncle Sam. Now the E. E. seniors each morning give the short Mr. Harris a stretching by means of holding his neck and pulling his feet. Under this treatment, in due time the three-quarters of an inch shall no doubt be acquired in one way or another, and then Centreville and Frostburg will be proud of Ensign George S. Harris, U. S. N.

name: Hoffman, William Samuel
motto: "He might be a very clever man by nature for all I know, but he laid so many books upon his head that his brains could not move."—Robert Hall.
description: HOFFMAN is another example to prove the law of inverse ratio between the size of the body and the capacity of the brain—Mechanic being the exception. He is five feet, three and seven-eighths inches tall with a weekly growth of one-sixteenth of an inch—regularly approaching this limit until Friday night, at which time it is gently removed in anticipation of his usual visit to the movies. (He believes himself to be an accurate scientist. We hope the above data has done him justice.) As for torso and appendages he is opaque, but his brain is quite clear, in fact we may even say trans—transcendent. Ankles weak, head-strong—that is Hoffman. Now turn him over and look at him from another angle. In the class room he is a shark par excellence at that delicate game of nine an' tens. He seems to be equally at home in any subject, as a result of that efficient system of his, which may be described in one word—work, whether it be a steady mental grind or helping Ma transport great stacks of books from the library to her lecture room and then returning them at the close of the hour. Hoffman is fast out-growing this latter tendency—especially since the geology trip and when he does we feel sure that one could hardly find a better fellow or one who will be of more credit to his alma mater.

name: Henry Hinkson Hopkins
motto: "He held his voice—a friend to the human race."
description: But he occasionally goes counter clockwise to the dope. Is Henry Hinksonian Hopkins ever hurried? Absolutely no. He is calm and collected at all times and on every occasion. He has the double distinction of having appeared in two successive publications of the HULLABALOO. H. H. H. appeared last year in order to make the book more beautiful (which he did) and this year to make the book most beautiful which he also did. Once we gave him a yell and the part of the HULLABALOO yell where we say "Hopkins" and then repeat the man's name three times was so confusing that poor, old Henry Hinkson didn't even know that he was being yelled for. He therefore missed a great moment of his life. However, whenever Hopkins hears a HULLABALOO now he smiles and blushes for who knows it may be a yell pro and on behalf of Henry Hinkson himself.

name: Suter, Jacob
motto: "We read that an ass's head was lately sold for eighty pieces of silver; and yet they were never more plentiful."—Swift.
description: He is well-named. Not, as you may suppose, from the thickness of his cranium (?) or any prominent assiferous projections, but from the mere and deplorable fact that he is a student. Why, he even knows his assignment the morning after a cotillon. Aside from his studies Bony is very energetic. He has a "peck" of girls, among whom Laura and Thekla seem to be in the lead. From the number of trips he makes into the suburbs he apparently has a girl on each of the Ten Hills. This corresponds exactly to the name "suitor," which is merely a mis-spelled form of the original. We do not hesitate to recommend Suter to any of the fair sex who desire a modern Apollo. He is musical, yet quiet. He is neat to the point of being exquisite. And sh-sh-h-h—he can cook. We have never known him to break any dishes and only once has he ruined a meal. Bony is an expert upon making electrical connections—often shoving aside his partners with the exclamation, "Get out of the way and let me do it." He is very good at getting marks and was once heard to say to one of the professors, "Doctor, you know that I know this subject. I have just got to have a ten and you only gave me a nine."

name: Edwin Bosley Jarrett
motto: "With words of learned length and thundering sound Amaz'd the gazing rustics ranged around."—Goldsmith.
description: By heck—here's Rube. Just four years ago Rube pulled his feet down from the flour barrel at the Jarrettsville Emporium, pulled on his best boots, and came up to the university—amid the applause of his fellow townsmen. Likewise according to Tiff, when "Down Home" returns for the holidays he is met at the station by the Firemen's Volunteer Band and escorted to the Public Square for appropriate ceremonies. This ovation is probably due to that same manifestation of public spirit and interest at home that has been so prominent in his career. In his senior year Rube, under the gentle guidance of the coach, managed our first football team which was composed largely of undergraduates. For his success in this direction he was later awarded the manager's letter—the H. M. During the past year, furthermore, the Student Council has greatly enlarged its field of activity, which has been largely due to Rube's competent administration. Our close association with the "Pride of All Jarrettsville" during the past four years has led us to believe that he intends to be a doctor—in fact we know that he will study medicine. At this juncture, however, in the exercise of that cherished editorial privilege of counselling, we would like to suggest to Rube the possibility of bringing his natural talents under the spotlight in some famous rural vehicle—such as "The Old Homestead" or a partnership with "Chic" Sales in the "Passing Show."

name: Kaufman, Herman Reed
motto: "My life is like a stroll upon the beach."—Thoreau.
clubs: prominent member of the track team
description: When it comes to writing up "Skinny" we were confronted with a hard problem for he has kept himself apart from the class during his entire stay with us. No one has been able to find anything on him and so we will be compelled to pass him up as a hard working, industrious student. He has taken some interest in athletics, and for the first three years of his stay with us he was a prominent member of the track team. During his last year, however, he gave it up in order to pay more attention to a former Virginia lady, now residing in his home town—Westminster. By heck, "Skinny" is also a musician of no mean ability, and during his senior year became the leader of the musical clubs. We hope that in our reunions in the future the class will find "Skinny" changed into a more congenial person and as Canton has said, "One who will give us a smile once in a while."

name: Johann Hinrich Knoop
motto: "The enemy is within our gates."
description: It is rumored that Knoop had much respect for the "Fatherland" until he took Political Science I. There, under the able tutelage of Dr. Millspaugh, he was taught of the horrible slavery to which the Germans are subjected. Of course, being able to read German he had seen the "Verboten" signs scattered promiscuously throughout the Kaiser's realms, but he had never understood their terrible significance. But now the poor fellow realizes how terrible it all was and, alas, is. But having tasted democracy, or rather having learned about it from Millspaugh, he will return to Germany after the war and start a revolution or something on that order. Still we cheerfully admit that it is hard to see in this quiet, amiable fellow the "terrible Hun." They say "scratch the Russian and you will find the Tartar." Well we—the whole editorial board—have assiduously scratched Johann Hinrich, but to no avail. He wouldn't even get sore. Of course, he exhibits all the horrors or most of the horrors of Kultur—wears stiff collars, drinks beer, etc.—but that is the only objection. Doc says drinking beer is no objection; perhaps he is right. But seriously we all like Knoop immensely and wish him the best of luck. If we were not at war we'd propose for him the German toast—"Hoch sollst du leben."

name: Bernard Louis Benno Kohn
description: A peculiar little fellow into Polycon did trip With his watch in his mouth and his hand on his hip— With a frowsy towsled head— With a nose that’s always red. He hemmed and he hawed and he whistled and he sang, He dropped his books with an awful bang; And he turned around to grin, With his knee underneath his chin. B. Kohn, B. Kohn, Distinctive and alone, The things we see in Polycon Three Denote some mental fallacy. They claim Your brain Is made of solid bone. Can this be true concerning you? B. Kohn, B. Kohn.

name: Konze, John Edwin
motto: "He's very like a cock as thinks the sun's rose o' purpose to hear him crow."—George Eliot.
description: "Ed" is one of those gifted persons who knows how to get away with it. Of course, much of his success, as he meekly acknowledges, is due to the fact that he does everything according to a definite program. "Everything that I do is performed according to a schedule. I sleep, eat and play according to my carefully outlined plan. Gentlemen, I must have my recreation (terrific applause)." We do not doubt that he follows this schedule, but from what we have seen it must run something like this: Get up at nine o'clock in time to attend an eighty-thirty class; smoke a half dozen stogies in the major's office; attend a class, perhaps; then get some chocolates and cigarettes for his afternoon visit to the major. Sally will tell you what he does with his evenings. The remainder of the time Ed writes up "lab" reports. He has majored not only in R. O. T. C. work but has also spent considerable time in E. E. class rooms. The strenuous work which has been thrust upon him has so undermined his health that we marvel how even his 7¾ size head has been able to master it. Not even Bony's insistent efforts have succeeded in causing him to "let up" at any time. Keep it up and you may be a real soldier some day.

name: Lampe, John Harold
motto: "Thy various follies, who can trace? Self, love, ambition, envy, pride Their empire in your heart divide." —Swift.
description: John Harold is one of the bright spots in our class history. He was especially noticeable in the "Polycon" class where Dr. Wolman, after eliciting several answers which savored strongly of the Dark Ages, would call out as his last resort, "Mr. Lampe, will you please throw a little light on the subject?" Harold hails from the city, but Dame Rumor has it that upon his graduation he will do some research work in Pittsburgh. The principal object of his study will be the discovery of a twin cylinder voltmeter, which will withstand three hundred volts on its one and a half volt scale. The reasons for his choosing Pittsburgh are many and varied. They range all the way from blondes to brunettes. On the Senior Engineering trip in April, Harold decided to sally forth to Wilkensburg for a dance. As bodyguard he took three officers of the University Battalion—with the result that two were left to their fate in Wilkensburg, while he and the remaining one double-timed up the railroad track only to be captured in Copeland. Judging from past performances, we can safely say that research will not occupy all of his time.

name: Raymond Earl Lenhard
motto: "Modesty is a becoming ornament to a young man." - Plautus.
description: RAYMOND has enjoyed the honor of being a member of our august body for but one year, and more the pity for "our august body" because Lenhard has contributed no little to its "augustity." We suspect that it was not for any love of us that he decided to graduate this year; nor was it because he found it convenient to do four years' work in three; such trifles are of no concern to him. The reason was that he wished to escape the task of being treasurer of the Junior class. Lenhard has had that job from birth (of the class of course) and was threatened with it for the years to come. We cannot blame him for this move for every one knows that collecting money from undergraduates whether for books, bets or bottles is no easy job. It is also interesting to note that our class, as well as the Juniors, has never awarded the position of treasurer to a man who has studied "Money and Banking" under "Tubby." This may be merely a coincidence—but we doubt it. Besides acting as treasurer, Lenhard has developed into a zoologist of note—spending much of his time in the field collecting specimens; but as Walter recently remarked, "Doc, there's plenty of 'em around here." All of which makes us rather uneasy about the welfare of Dick Fields and some of our other interesting specimens.

name: Levy, Isidore
motto: "Girls and a good understanding—what else could a man need."
description: THERE is Levy, student of chemistry. He has long confessed that his chief research will be the discovering of some compound which will so preserve the condition of the face that he and Michelson will no longer have to visit Walter's tonsorial establishment twice daily. Walter grows weak in the knees whenever he is told to remove that large growth of black shrubbery from Levy's face; and as soon as he removes it, it is back again. Still, the never-dying smile which is ever illuminating his noble countenance, almost hides the Bolshevikian beard, so that he is not much the worse for it. That smile is the key to his character. Friendly, jovial, congenial to all—especially to those who owe him money—he has won many friends and no enemies, except, perhaps, Snowball, the swarthy dirtchaser on the second floor of Gilman Hall, who says that he will prevent Levy's graduating, unless he pays him the fifteen cents which he borrowed from him last year. All in all, Levy is a good sport, quiet, but persistent in his studies, and well liked by all—especially the ladies, as anyone can testify who has seen him in his daily promenade down Lexington Street. May he achieve the greatest success in his work in chemistry, which he intends to resume next year.

name: Lester Stern Levy
motto: "Whence is thy learning? Hath thy toil O'er books consumed the midnight oil." — Swift.
description: We maintain that just as one would never suspect by beholding Adolfus Gustavus Hamburger that he was connected with a haberdashery somewhere on Baltimore street, one could never guess by looking at Lester after May fifteenth that he was a descendant of a ROYAL line of straw hat manufacturers. If Lester is seen this year after May fifteenth, however, he will be wearing the felt hat of Uncle Sam instead of the usual dilapidated straw with the usual colored hat-band displaced by the buff hat cord. Lester is a man of weaknesses—among those that can be classed as minor may be mentioned reckless speeding and Jeff Miller. Among Lester's major weaknesses are French (especially the French comedy in the unexpurgated editions) and Jeff Miller. We will not recall Lester's other failings; they are too complex, and, besides, he is our friend; but suffice it to say that among these latter is Jeff Miller. Lester is a good talker, and would make an able politician. When the war is over—provided Lester does not remain in Paris to study French and French comediennes for the remainder of his life—he might even get a seat in the City Council. We feel that he is well equipped with all the vices of a city politician, in that he is a capable speaker and a close associate of Jeff Miller. We know he would achieve success.

name: William Samuel Love, Jr.
motto: "Studies? Let all such things slide!"
description: If the Class of '18 can boast of having within its ranks any "cats" or "wild birds," "Doc" Love is there. William S. entered the university a demure little freshman from City College, and ever since things have been going from bad to worse. The Geology trip last spring capped the climax. As a member of the so-called "Horned Five," of which Luther Houghton and "Teddy" Thurman were also components, "Doc" left his tracks in every Western Maryland town visited, and upon reaching Cumberland literally painted the town red. As a matter of course, he spent much of his evenings owling about, and, consequently, when Dr. Swartz would set out on a climb the next day we would invariably find "Doc" prone to cast himself by the wayside, or carefully seclude himself beneath a large rock to avoid questions or note-taking. Notwithstanding the above minor considerations, however, "Doc" has a great many redeeming traits. Besides being a skillful artist with the bones, and the mainstay of the Basketball Team, he is also an ardent baseball supporter. His chief amusements are recorded above—with one exception—cutting class. He is an inveterate cutter, and the peculiar part is that he always gets away with it—and with good marks, too, for when there is something to be done well, "Doc" does it well. We understand from entirely reliable sources that "Doc" has aspirations for an M. D., and will, therefore, enter the Medical School next fall. With this decision we are perfectly content, for we know that his ability applied along this line will some day make us proud of him as a member of '18.

name: Morris Abel Mechanic
motto: "Mens sana in corpore sano."
clubs: Member Menorah Society, F., So., S.
description: Poor Mechanic has had a hard time of it—especially in the beginning. For this fine specimen of manhood got much more than his rightful share of hazing. He was the only freshman whom "Willie" could beat; and hazed he was. At any time, at any place, for any reason or for no reason whatsoever, "Willie" worried Mechanic about regulations which, as far as we could discover, never even existed. But the finished product—the physical and mental marvel—is worth all the trouble which it took to produce him. We do not know what he intends to do after graduation—he is so modest and demure—but we hope that it will be nothing in which he would get a chance to get back at "Willie." For we suspect that even this little fellow has a soul, and who knows that behind this sweet child-like countenance there might lurk a revengeful spirit. The one university activity in which Mechanic condescended to indulge is characteristic of him. The International Economics Association of America remains unorganized, simply because of the ignorance and lack of imagination of his classmates. But genius will out, and prophesy Mechanic will some day grow a beard.

name: Merryman, Lewis
motto: "What's in a name."—Shakespeare.
description: MERRYMAN is several things. In the first place, he is twenty-three; in the second place, he is soon going into the service; in the third, fourth and sixth places, he is a student, economist and logician. We omitted the fifth place because it is something that we do not know much about, namely, and to wit, he is a ladies' man. One of the fair "damozels" is called Helen, and, according to present indications, poor Merryman is going to do the Paris stunt, and bring about the fall of Troy. Then no longer will he be "the Merryman, with happy face and coat of tan" (note rhyme), but he will be morose, miserable and mournful. Merryman has Bolsheviki blood in his arteries—although you would not suspect it to look at him. The family name before his grandfather, Jacob Hotthold, came to these parts was Froelichermann. The origin of such things as Froelichermann can be immediately recognized by any of the budding, or, rather, blooming philologists of Dr. Roulston's classes. The best place for Merryman to enlist would be in the Economical Department, U. S. A. Why? Because that's his major subject, and "Tubby" Barnett will tell anybody that Merryman is his most economical of economists, methodical of statisticians and financial of "Money & Bankers." So help me Jevons and Taussig!

name: Israel David Michelson
motto: "When I think of my past I wonder what I shall do among the angels, where they sing and pray all the time."
description: You have seen him coming down the driveway with that peculiar sort of limp of his, his head slightly on one side, his teeth supporting feebly a cigarette, and his eyes half closed. Haven't you looked at him and felt like asking him whether his girl had thrown him down, or how much the escapade of the night before cost him in sleep and money? To look at him you would think he were a misanthrope of the worst sort. But talk to him once and hear his loud and unrestrained laughter, and your fears are soon dispersed. Don't worry, Mike is no man-hater—unless it is his barber. Nor is he a woman-hater—his host of girl friends will testify to that. Mike is going to be a physician, whether he wants to or not. His family decided that for him long ago; they began to call him Doc when he was about ten years old. Altogether, he is a pretty decent sort of a fellow. His morals, it is true, have been lately shaken somewhat from too frequent contact with Wells. But it is hoped that he will soon get over that. We expect him to make a good professional man, because, despite the fact that he spends a good deal of his evenings and early mornings in gay life, he is conscientious in his work. And that is sufficient.

name: Charles Herman Miegel
motto: "If he was as wise as he looks he would be a wonder."---Addison.
description: Poor Mary's dead! Mary in her youthful days joined a Roman fraternity for insurance purposes only. She was a mere slip of a girl, and had a very hard home life. She was a good risk---at least all the fellows said so---and it was not difficult for her to pay monthly dividends. At the age of sixteen she gave an insurance inspector the premium, and is now serving a term policy. But Miegel, who had been observing the case carefully for its practical value in his study of economics, and who also knew the ins and outs of Mary, thought that her expectation of life was extremely good. But once, while measuring her standard deviation, he noticed that her smooth curve was skewed, and on appealing to Dr. Barnett, found out that fire insurance and infant mortality had inverse correlation. Despite Charlie's statistical gymnastics, he was awarded a rubber gold-fish at a shooting party in his boyhood days, and as an American soldier is a great inspiration to the dear old folks at home. In all the Senior debates the first speaker always announced that the man in the full-dress shirt and ingrown collar seated at his left was not a waiter, but the advisor of the Fourth Year Bullsheviki. But this is all wrong, all wrong; for Charlie is not only the benefactor of little Mary Ann Uity, but the friend of all other economical and limited payment girls.

name: Miller, Jay Jefferson
motto: "Soc et tu'em."
description: The editors wish to apologize for the liberties that they have taken with the above quotation. This version, which best describes the activities of our energetic business manager, is advanced as the true and more modern version of an entirely obsolete phrase which was taken up in a certain course in linguistics on a certain Monday afternoon. Even Jeff, himself, admits that the introduction of the above version caused the professor in charge to become very excited, and to make a hurried and unintelligible comment, which sounded something like this: Wump, wump, woo, Verdie. And who would have thought that this innocent-looking lad could have stimulated the editors to make such a translation as this? Yet no one would suspect the sweet little boy who came here four years ago of being capable of some of the stunts which he pulls off. For instance, we were amazed to find that he takes two courses from Mr. Greene—he doesn't look like that kind of a fellow, does he? Well, the courses come in succession, and Jeff has it arranged so nicely that Ma always says at the end of the hour: "Walk out quietly, gentlemen, Mr. Miller sleeps here during the next hour also." Jeff intends to represent 1918 in the field of journalism, and we know of no one who could do it more ably or reflect more credit upon his class or university.

name: Morley, Frank Vigor
motto: "Smooth runs the water where the brook is deep."
clubs: Lieutenant Morley who is an instructor on the Staff
description: Take another look at this fellow—he’s a shark. Frank Vigor hails from Haverford from whence he came to join our class in the Senior Year. He jumped right in as soon as he landed, and it didn’t take long for us to find out that Morley was willing to undertake things, and exceedingly capable of putting them across in fine shape. If you want to know anything about mathematics, ask Morley, he’s a math. prof’s son, besides being a pillar of the Junior Mathematics Club. If you want to know something about Military Science and Tactics, ask Lieutenant Morley who is an instructor on the Staff—last October he was a private. As a member of the Senior Debating Team he is quite well remembered, for the skill with which he so thoroughly analyzed the arguments of his opponents rivaled that of a logician. Although Frank never had a lacrosse stick in his hands before this year, he now holds down the point’s job in an admirable manner, and his old familiar “Pull yourselves together, now boys, get together” will not soon be forgotten. Poised, modest and unassuming, sincere and deeply earnest, extending a warm and wholesome friendship to everyone alike, Frank Vigor Morley has won a place in the heart of every Hopkins man who knew him. With his brilliant mind and genial disposition we feel that his place in the world is assured, and we are proud to have him a member of 1918—every inch a gentleman and a man.

name: Cletus Edmund Mcquigan
motto: "Our unsteady actions cannot be managed by rules of strict philosophy."—Sir Robert Howard.
description: He requested the editors to seize this opportunity to make it emphatically known to the reading public that there is absolutely no German blood in his veins. How such a rumor could have gained credence we are at a loss to understand. There is nothing in Cletus' name to suggest the Teutonic. Miegel, a friend of "Mac" defends him thus: "Shall we accuse him of being Pennsylvania Dutch, because he hails from that State?" In short, we acquit "Mac" of having even the smallest number of German corpuscles in his blood. But alas! We cannot acquit "Mac" of being a gamester. Were he to confine himself to the honest and dignified game of poker, or to the harmless sport of matching nickels he would be one of us—a jolly good fellow. But no! Cletus rolls the bones! Why one day in Biology "lab" he deprived us of three iron men and two bits. There's the rub. We cannot sanction a man stooping to such a thing. "Mac" has another weak point besides taking German three; he is strikingly similar to Napoleon, Oscar Wilde, and Henry VIII in his predilection for the fair sex. In fact his friendship for Miegel and Wells and his admiration for Love (Doc) may be due to the wide circle of feminine acquaintances which these gentlemen are reputed to possess. The difference between "Mac" and these eminent gentlemen is that the former's affairs are not Platonic.

name: Neuhausen, Benjamin S.
motto: "Learning without thought is labor lost."—Confucius.
description: NEUHAUSEN is the class radical. His hair is always long; his face is unshaved; and his clothes are those of a genius. He speaks carelessly of Schopenhauer and Spinoza. He loves to "shock people." He is little disturbed when he angers the "all-powerful George" or the "loquacious Bismarck." He intends to earn his living as a chemist—but he has taken the study of "classic dancing as a profession." And finally unlike all the rest he appears to be very happy when the little tables are set up semi-annually in the Donovan Room.

name: Norris, Grover William
description: With his hair parted neatly When he smiles, oh! so sweetly, He looks like a mischievous lad. NORRIS came to the university on one of those recent spring days with his pallid countenance deeply flushed, with that usual steady vacant stare now interpolated with short furtive glances. In fact the man who before had "looked the whole world in the face and feared not any man" now seemed nervous, timid, and evasive. What could the trouble be? What could Norris have done? Finally a small group decided to ask—we thought that we had a right to know if he had begun to hang out with the Wells or "Doc" Love combination. He first resorted to evasive answers, and then, seeing that there was no escape, he turned to us in one of those confidential moods and said in a voice trembling with emotion, "Fellows, I've decided to do it. In the future I shall spell through, t-h-r-u, and though, t-h-o." This was the most radical thing he ever did in all his college career. The remainder of the time he has spent in close communication with Horace and Plautus, where he was perfectly safe from all the evil influences of this cruel world. We almost wish that he had been a little more inclined to be friendly, not to say intimate with his classmates; for we feel that we have lost much by his aloofness. In your career, Norris, as an instructor, we wish you the best of luck and we hope others may be more successful in getting to know you than we were.

name: O'Neill, George Devine
motto: "Man delight not me—no nor woman either."—Hamlet.
description: "Pat" is a very busy student—typifying industry itself. Whenever seen he is always on the run for he makes use of every available moment. He has developed into a writer of no mean ability judging from some of the volumes he has submitted as reports to experiments and incidentally he has given his instructors undue trouble in attempting to decipher his hieroglyphics. He also is the champion note taker of the class—and as for accuracy the sixth an accurate result. Although prudent in every other respect he violates the one great natural law—the law of gravity—for he refuses to keep his feet on the floor when drawing. His chief diversions are reading the notices on the military bulletin board and discussing the war situation. "Pat" is rather tall and often on surveying parties, while standing in the distance with his arms extended, has given undue concern to transitmen who find it difficult to distinguish him from the telegraph poles in the background. We understand that he is aspiring to become a railroad engineer and we sincerely hope that he will be as successful in this as he was in his student life in the university.

name: Rosenthal, Jacob Stanley
motto: "This fellow seems to possess but one idea and that the wrong one."
clubs: Knights of the Lathe and Transit
description: This fellow seems to possess but one idea and that the wrong one. ROSIE—one of the leading spirits of the Knights of the Lathe and Transit—has astonished many of us with his rare literary accomplishments. This talent was carefully nursed by the poetic Janney in the "slopover" and last year when the first copy of "La Bullslingaire" was published under the supervision of Rosie, the Doc felt amply rewarded. Bullslingaires are now always quite plentiful here at Hopkins. The championship of the indoor meet, in which the main events are chess and checkers and the main contestants are Rosenthal, O'Neill, and Weil, is the only thing which has ever given Rosie the slightest anxiety. This anxiety is, however, uncalled for, as we are confident that not one of the three will ever arrive at any decision other than that he himself should be proclaimed as champion. When it comes to bridge computations, furthermore, the honors also go to this trio. We never could quite see how they could compute to seventeen places on their slide rules. Rosie expects some day to be a civil engineer—from what we have seen that will be some day.

name: James Everett Sanner
motto: "The mildest manners with the bravest mind."—Homer.
clubs: Chairman Committee Arranging Inter-class Debate, S.
description: SANNER is the "silent man" of the class. Modest, retiring, and a man of few words—such a man is Sanner. In consequence he is not widely known about the university, yet his classmates will always remember him as the efficient chairman of the class debates and banquets. He is usually seen only in one place about the university—the biological laboratory. Here he is wont to dissect cats—big cats, small cats, alley cats and pet cats—such is his pastime. On one occasion when a new consignment of cats had arrived, Sanner after surveying the group made his selection and forthwith began his dissection. It was not long, however, before he breathlessly reported to the instructor that he had not chosen a cat but cats. All of which goes to show that not even a champion cat dissector is infallible. It is reported that under certain given conditions—e. g., a class banquet, "Doc" Love, and a phone call—even the "silent man" has been known to break into jesting; it being further reported that at punning he shows himself to be a master. He is at once an ace and a joker, feeling now like a king and now like the deuce. Usually at this gentle art of punning he has the whole board to himself and leads out a row of trumps. We, however, feel that in spite of this punning capacity or probably because of it he should stick to medicine. It is said that his idea of what constitutes a good doctor was inspired by what Mephistopheles said of a physician's privilege and advantages.

name: Sapp, Charles Daniel
motto:
"With various readings stored his empty skull,
Learned, without sense, and venerably dull."
—Churchill.
description: SAPP says he is a minister—yet he has up to the present writing never been able to convince any of his fellow-students of this fact. His main curriculum activity has been taking courses in English and history; his extra-curriculum activities have been confined solely to browsing around in the history "stacks." Yet he was awarded last year Mechanical medal for bravery, not under fire, in recognition of his act in forcing his way single handed into No Man's Land—the Y. W. C. A. Here we are told that he has given without stint of his time and energy. All of which is probably true for he has devoted little of it to university activities. It is said that he has banded together the old ladies' branch into a Crocheting League, which from time to time donates to the various war committees many useful articles—just at present it is crocheting a muffler for Senator Chamberlain. With regard to characteristics and qualifications need we say more than that he has every requirement for his life work—the ministry. Wishing him success and good luck under such circumstances we feel would be useless.

name: David Petit Smith
motto: "Let a fool hold his tongue and he will pass as a saint." - Voltaire.
description: Here's a fellow who could found several mathematical and physical libraries merely on what he knows if it were put into book form. Whenever he is seen about the university he is always engaged in delivering some lengthy explanation. At the end of his intricate and unusually complete answer one need only have it published and the book would sell for two dollars net. In fact several fellows have worked their way through the university just by asking him questions. Davie is too much of a student, too backward, too retiring for most of his classmates. It is his sole delight to study. Although such an ambition is not to be too strongly criticized, we know that he has missed much by devoting all of his time to calculus and vector analysis. His main extra curriculum activities have been dilating on the advantages of living in Centreville and airing his tenor on the university Glee Club in a way which has not been heard since the days of McDowell.

name: Spiers, Thomas Hoskins
motto: "The honor is overpaid, When that did the act is commentator." —Shirley.
description: We have heard somewhere that he considers himself the champion open field dancer of the cotillon club as well as an experienced critic of all forms of the terpsichorean art—being one of the few to really appreciate the reproductions in "The Land of Joy" of such rarities as "the equado" and "the fandango," which are rarely seen outside of Barcelona itself. It is entirely possible to understand how one who has learned to interpret the expression of the Spanish temperament in both prose and poetry could be equally in accord with the expression of that same temperament in the various dances characteristic of the little country "in the corner of Europe." We reiterate that an experienced critic he may be—that we have no means of determining, but an experienced dancer he is not—of that we are confident. We understand that Spiers intends to enter eventually the diplomatic corps. If that course is pursued we are confident that under this training he will be better able to understand human nature, he will learn to think less of himself and begin to appreciate the value of associating with (other) human beings.

name: Wasserman, Harry
motto: "There is method in his madness."
description: At the end of a long successful career these words will be on Harry's epitaph. Medicine every one knows is his strong point. And some physician Harry is going to be. Can you imagine him in a sumptuously furnished office, the walls of which are entirely yet systematically covered with pictures of movie actresses; and Dr. Harry himself, with brown side-whiskers and mustache, leaning back in his office chair gazing up at a beautiful screen star for inspiration?* Harry before entering the university was one of the managing editors of the "System" magazine. He gave up that position to show the world that even a college student may be systematic in his actions. And he certainly has succeeded in proving his point. Not only is he systematic in his studying, eating and sleeping, but even in his patronage of moving-picture theatres—which is enormous. He has arranged a system for himself whereby he may see certain actresses—never actors—at certain theatres on certain nights. The details of the system are unknown for he never takes any one—neither man nor lady—with him to his sanctum sanctorum—the movies. When it becomes time to apply his system to his patients, we feel that he will be just as successful as he was in college—a source of great pride to the university and to '18. * We cannot.—Editors.

name: Weil, Joseph
motto: "He takes more care to hide his wisdom than his folly."--Swift.
description: He takes more care to hide his wisdom than his folly. WHISKERS WEIL attributes his fame to the black luxuriant beard that always adorns his face—for no matter how clean shaven Joe tries to be, the whiskers are always there. Joe also has another claim to fame in his ability as a chess and checker player. He impressed the class with this when he made his smashing clean-up in the chess tournament of the engineering class last year. Furthermore (unknown to himself) he possesses quite some literary talent, for as an associate editor of "La Bullslingaire" he made a number of noteworthy contributions. Weil is also quite daring when it comes to setting style. The class will never forget the scare of the lavender socks nor of the polka dot ties. Before finally deciding upon the engineering profession, Weil threatened his family with becoming first a psychologist, then changed to sanitary engineering and finally settled upon the field of electricity because of the "opportunities which are afforded for extensive research." We sincerely hope that he will make as good an engineer as he has made a congenial companion.

name: Wells, William John H. B.
clubs: Member Musical Clubs, So., J., S.
description: Wells is a loafer, He has a vicious mien ; Duty here and pleasure there And John Henry's in between. He studies little, as you know, Because it's such a snap So just go to—, I'll smoke my pipe And drink what e'er's on tap. WELL, "Willie," how's the Palace, or the Follies, or the Gayety, or Holliday Street? All you have to do is to give Wellsie a chance and away he goes from the end of one show to another. In other words, our friend Wells is a kind of sport—a theatrical man—so to speak. In his weekly rounds, he not only takes in the above-mentioned institutions, but also deigns to frequent such places as Ford's or the Academy. With all due respect to the intellectual attainments of our friend, it is, however, a well known fact that theatrics is his major subject and women his minor. Willie's special friend in the faculty is his adviser, Doc Andrews. They get along beautifully—Willie has Doc advise him what courses to take, and then turns around and does exactly as he pleases about it. The only time a shadow is ever cast across his path is in February and June. No sooner do the language profs finish bawling him out for being a "clever crammer" for exams than his friend Andrews boards him about getting higher marks in languages than he does in his major subject—Biology—so there you are be damned, says Willie. We all like Wellsie though—why shouldn't we, he's a good scout, and when we hold our class reunion twenty years from now, we expect to find him near the top of the ladder—and still going up.

name: Winslow, Oliver Parry
motto: "The keen spirit seizes the prompt occasion and makes the thought start into instant action."—Hannah More.
description: Make way, ye crowds! Make way! Here comes the august president of '18. Compared with Winslow's task of getting those wild engineers and frenzied "academics" to pull together, Wilson's duties as a war President seem trifling. No assembly was more split on party lines than was our class in the meetings we held early last fall. Now behold us—one harmonious whole—due mainly to the tact and good work of our president. After Winslow's election we heard one man say—an academic of course—"Well that is the best joke St. John's has put over on us yet." Later we all agreed that whatever else we might say about St. John's, we must thank her for developing "Monk." But aside from his official life we have found him to be a great fellow and he may feel assured that in future years he'll always have the best wishes of 1918. To him—now in the uniform of his country—we propose the toast—"over the top with best o' luck."

name: Woodward, Richard Henley
motto: "Is mihi videtur amplissimus qui sua virtute in altiorem locum pervenit."—Cicero.
description: What a lot we have to say about you, Dick—and such little space to say it. Did you ever see him play football or lacrosse? Well, it was he—"Tom cat" Woodward—who this year led the scrappiest team we have ever put upon the gridiron. When a freshman, he also landed a defense job on the lacrosse team, and many a time we have heard him "come through" with that old, characteristic "whoop" in bringing home the bacon for his Alma Mater. Dick is a worker—when he wants to be—and he has the ability. Don't get the impression that he is a "ten man," because he isn't—nor does he flunk any courses. As a geologist particularly his fame is widespread, for, according to Dr. Swartz, he was about to revolutionize the science of Geology by finding a certain fossil in an unheard of place. However, upon investigation, it was shown that Dick had dropped his fossil, which he had brought from home, in the wrong place. Modest, calm, sincerely earnest, Dick Woodward has served his class and University as few men have. He is a good example of the real spirit of '18, and our sincerest good wishes go with him as he enters the services of the nation; for we know that he will shed glory upon that branch which he has entered, as he has shed glory through his conduct, upon his class and University.

name: Louis Morris Zeskind
description: The man of mystery. He cometh like a ghost in the night and departeth as soon as his classes are finished. His ways are not our ways and (we hope) his thoughts are not our thoughts. At the very end of the biographies the editors are completely stumped. Who he is, what he is or even why he is? We haven't the slightest idea. Careful investigation shows that he was born, became a freshman, and will graduate. More we cannot say. He is good in his studies and works hard and we feel sure that he must be saving his latent powers for something important—surely he has not wasted them on '18. Well, Mr. Zeskind (we don't know you well enough to address you otherwise), we wish you the best of luck in your profession. When you do open your mouth—but what's the use of waiting for the Sphinx to speak—the HULLABALOO must be first written.
